en.extract:
Stejneger's scoter (Melanitta stejnegeri), also known as the Siberian scoter, is a large sea duck. The genus name is derived from Ancient Greek melas "black" and netta "duck".


Taxonomy
Stejneger's scoter was described by the American ornithologist Robert Ridgway in 1887 and given the binomial name Oidemia stejnegeri. The specific name was chosen to honour the Norwegian born ornithologist Leonhard Stejneger.
It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the white-winged scoter. It was suggested to be a full species, according to a new study, and the species were split in 2019.


Description

There are a number of differing characteristics of Stejneger's scoter and the white-winged scoter. Males of the white-winged scoter have browner flanks, dark yellow coloration of most of the bill and a less tall bill knob, approaching the velvet scoter. The male Stejneger's scoter has a very tall knob at the base of its mostly orange-yellow bill. The males of both species are very similar and best distinguished by head shape; White-winged scoters tend to have "two-stepped" profile between the bill and the head, compared to the long "Roman nose" profile of Stejneger's scoter similar to that of a common eider. Additionally, the feathering along the base of the upper mandible forms a right angle on the white-winged scoter, compared to the acute angle on the Stejneger's scoter.


Distribution
Stejneger's scoter breeds over the far north of Asia east of the Yenisey Basin. It winters further south in temperate zones, in Asia as far south as China. It forms large flocks on suitable coastal waters. These are tightly packed, and the birds tend to take off together.
Though previously considered rare in North America, Stejneger's scoter may be a regular visitor and possible breeder in Western Alaska, with sightings in Nome, Saint Paul Island and Gambell. A Stejneger's scoter was seen in Helena Valley, Montana, between April and May 2015, the only confirmed North American occurrence of the species south of Alaska. A scoter seen in Monterey Bay, California, on January 15, 2014, appeared to have characteristics consistent with Stejneger's scoter but the record was not accepted by the committee due to the poor quality of images. Stejneger's scoter is also a rare vagrant to Europe and has been recorded from France, Finland, Poland,  Iceland, Ireland, Norway, Scotland and Sweden.


Behaviour and ecology


Feeding
In freshwater, this species primarily feeds on crustaceans and insects; while in saltwater areas, it feeds on molluscs and crustaceans.


References


External links

BirdLife species factsheet for Melanitta stejnegeri
Interactive range map of Melanitta stejnegeri at IUCN Red List

'''Stejneger's scoter''' ('''''Melanitta stejnegeri'''''), also known as the '''Siberian scoter''', is a large [[Merginae|sea duck]]. The genus name is derived from [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc-Latn|melas}} "black" and {{Lang|grc-Latn|netta}} "duck".

==Taxonomy==
Stejneger's scoter was described by the American ornithologist [[Robert Ridgway]] in 1887 and given the [[binomial name]] ''Oidemia stejnegeri''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Ridgway | first=Robert | author-link=Robert Ridgway | year=1887 | title=A Manual of North American Birds | location=Philadelphia | publisher=Lippincott | page=112 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7567329 }}</ref> The specific name was chosen to honour the Norwegian born ornithologist [[Leonhard Stejneger]].<ref>{{cite web | last=Jobling | first=J.A. | year=2019 | title=''stejnegeri''  | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive: Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/definition/stejnegeri | access-date=24 June 2019 }}</ref>

It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[white-winged scoter]]. It was suggested to be a full species, according to a new study, and the species were split in 2019.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/siberian-scoter-melanitta-stejnegeri|title=Siberian Scoter (Melanitta stejnegeri)|publisher=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=White-winged Scoter overview |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/White-winged_Scoter/overview |access-date=2025-11-08 |website=All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Melanitta stejnegeri 82174730.jpg|thumb|Female with young in [[Tuva]], Russia]]
There are a number of differing characteristics of Stejneger's scoter and the [[white-winged scoter]]. Males of the white-winged scoter have browner flanks, dark yellow coloration of most of the bill and a less tall bill knob, approaching the velvet scoter. The male Stejneger's scoter has a very tall knob at the base of its mostly orange-yellow bill. The males of both species are very similar and best distinguished by head shape; White-winged scoters tend to have "two-stepped" profile between the bill and the head, compared to the long "Roman nose" profile of Stejneger's scoter similar to that of a [[common eider]].<ref name="Garner">{{cite web |author=Martin Garner |year=2014 |title=Velvet, White-winged and Stejneger's Scoters: A Photographic Guide |url=https://www.birdguides-cdn.com/cdn/LegacyBirdwatchArticles/velvet%20and%20white-winged%20scoters%20id%20photo%20guide_birdwatchfeb14lr.pdf |access-date=23 December 2018 |publisher=Birdwatch}}</ref> Additionally, the feathering along the base of the upper mandible forms a right angle on the white-winged scoter, compared to the acute angle on the Stejneger's scoter.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Reeber |first=Sébastien |title=Waterfowl of North America, Europe, and Asia: an identification guide |date=2015 |isbn=978-0-691-16266-9 |location=Princeton, NJ |oclc=927363728}}</ref>

==Distribution==
Stejneger's scoter breeds over the far north of [[Asia]] east of the [[Yenisey]] Basin. It [[bird migration|winters]] further south in [[temperate]] zones, in Asia as far south as China. It forms large flocks on suitable coastal waters. These are tightly packed, and the birds tend to take off together.

Though previously considered rare in North America, Stejneger's scoter may be a regular visitor and possible breeder in Western Alaska, with sightings in [[Nome, Alaska|Nome]], [[Saint Paul Island (Alaska)|Saint Paul Island]] and [[Gambell, Alaska|Gambell]]. A Stejneger's scoter was seen in Helena Valley, Montana, between April and May 2015, the only confirmed North American occurrence of the species south of Alaska. A scoter seen in Monterey Bay, California, on January 15, 2014, appeared to have characteristics consistent with Stejneger's scoter but the record was not accepted by the committee due to the poor quality of images.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Benson |first1=Thomas A. |last2=Fowler |first2=Rob |last3=McCaskie |first3=Guy |last4=Stahl |first4=Justyn T. |date=2020-07-31 |title=The 44th Annual Report of the California Bird Records Committee: 2018 Records |url=https://www.westernfieldornithologists.org/archive/V51/51(3)-p228-p260.pdf |journal=Western Birds |volume=51 |issue=3 |pages=228–260 |doi=10.21199/WB51.3.4|s2cid=225352840 }}</ref> Stejneger's scoter is also a rare vagrant to Europe and has been recorded from France, Finland, Poland,<ref name = BF>{{cite web |url=https://birdingfrontiers.wordpress.com/2011/06/17/white-winged-scoter-taxonomy/ |title= White-winged scoter taxonomy |date=17 June 2011 |access-date=14 August 2023 |publisher=Birding Frontiers}}</ref>  Iceland, Ireland, Norway,<ref name = Garner/> Scotland<ref name = Gillon>{{cite web | url = https://www.birdguides.com/articles/britain-ireland/rarity-finders-stejnegers-scoter-in-lothian/ | title = Rarity finders: Stejneger's Scoter in Lothian | author = Keith Gillon | date = 5 February 2023 | access-date = 14 August 2023 | publisher = Birdguides}}</ref> and Sweden.<ref name = BLS>{{cite web | url = https://birdlife.se/rk/raritetskatalogen/gass-svanar-ander/sibirisk-knolsvarta/ | title = Sibirisk knölsvärta / Stejneger's Scoter | author = BirdLife Sverige | date = 2023 | access-date = 2 August 2025 | publisher = BirdLife Sverige}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
===Feeding===
In freshwater, this species primarily feeds on [[crustacean]]s and [[insect]]s; while in saltwater areas, it feeds on [[mollusc]]s and [[crustacean]]s.

zh.extract:
斑脸海番鸭（学名：Melanitta stejnegeri）为鸭科海番鸭属的鸟类，俗名海番鸭、奇嘴鸭。分布于西伯利亚东部、勘察加、鄂霍次克海、南至朝鲜、日本、中国东北及东部海域。该物种的模式产地在西伯利亚堪察加及日本。。多栖息于沿海海滩、内陆淡水河湖间以及湖沼地区。该物种原为绒海番鸭的西伯利亚亚种（学名：M. f. stejnegeri），物种拆分前的中文名亦为斑脸海番鸭。
